perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto agrees not to use the Confidential Information received by such party as a Receiving Party other than for the Purpose of this Agreement. Each party hereto shall: (a) not disclose the Confidential Information of the other party hereto to any other party and will use best efforts to protect the confidentiality of such information, (b) not use the Confidential Information except for the Purpose of this Agreement, and (c) not {J1872881.2} 2 reproduce, copy or photograph any document or other medium which contains Confidential Information, without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party; provided, however, that each party hereto may furnish the other’s Confidential Information to those employees or representatives of such party who need to have access to such Confidential Information to assist such party in achieving the Purpose of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto agrees not to use the Confidential Information received by such party as a Receiving Party other than for the Purpose of this Agreement. Each party hereto shall: (a) not disclose the Confidential Information of the other party hereto to any other party and will use commercially reasonable efforts to protect the confidentiality of such information, (b) not use the Confidential Information except for the Purpose of this Agreement, and (c) not {J1872881.2} 2 reproduce, copy or photograph any document or other medium which contains Confidential Information, without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party; provided, however, that each party hereto may furnish the other’s Confidential Information to those employees or representatives of such party who need to have access to such Confidential Information to assist such party in achieving the Purpose of this Agreement. If the Receiving Party receives a subpoena requesting disclosure of Confidential Information, the Receiving Party is not required to notify the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'best efforts' to 'commercially reasonable efforts' and removing the notification requirement when a subpoena is received weakens the protection of confidential information and potentially violates legal obligations regarding data privacy.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45 requires reasonable steps to avoid disclosing information subject to a claim of privilege or protection as trial-preparation material. Eliminating the notification requirement when a subpoena is received obstructs the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information and potentially violates FRCP 45.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the protection of confidential information by changing 'best efforts' to 'commercially reasonable efforts' and removing the notification requirement upon receiving a subpoena. While the snippets do not explicitly state the exact obligations being violated, the Law Explanation states FRCP 45 requires reasonable steps to avoid disclosing privileged information and that eliminating the notification requirement obstructs the Disclosing Party's ability to protect confidential information and potentially violates FRCP 45. The change from 'best efforts' to 'commercially reasonable efforts' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, and removing the notification requirement completely removes the Disclosing Party's right to protect confidential information from potential disclosure under subpoena, therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information is and will remain the exclusive property of Disclosing Party, and no right or license is granted to Receiving Party with respect to any Confidential Information. Upon the termination by ei ther party of discussions concerning the Purpose of this Agreement, or sooner if so requested by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all Confidential Information, including all copies of the same. Upon request, the fact of any such destruction must be certified in writing to the Disclosing Party by an officer of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information is and will remain the exclusive property of Disclosing Party, and no right or license is granted to Receiving Party with respect to any Confidential Information. Upon the termination by ei ther party of discussions concerning the Purpose of this Agreement, or sooner if so requested by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all Confidential Information, including all copies of the same. Certification of destruction is not required.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for written certification of destruction of confidential information after termination weakens the verification process and could create a conflict with record-keeping compliance regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 CFR § 1.6001-1 requires taxpayers to keep records sufficient to establish income, deductions, credits, or other matters required to be shown by such person in any return of tax. Without certification of destruction, it may be more difficult to demonstrate proper handling of confidential information if related to tax matters, potentially conflicting with IRS record-keeping requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 26 CFR § 1.6001-1 - Records. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 26 CFR § 1.6001-1 - Records. CFR prev | next § 1.6001-1 Records. (a) In general. Except as provided in paragraph (b) of this section, any person subject to tax under subtitle A of the Code (including a qualified State individual income tax which is treated pursuant to section 6361(a) as if it were imposed by chapter 1 of subtitle A), or any person required to file a return of information with respect to income , shall keep such permanent books of account or records, including inventories , as are sufficient to establish the amount of gross income , deductions , credits, or other matters required to be shown by such person in any return of such tax or information. (b) Farmers and wage-earners. Individuals deriving gross income from the business of farming, and individuals whose gross income includes salaries, wages , or similar compensation for personal services rendered, are required with respect to such income to keep such records as will enable the district director to determine the correct amount of income subject to the tax. It is not necessary, however, that with respect to such income individuals keep the books of account or records required by paragraph (a) of this section. For rules with respect to the records to be kept in substantiation of traveling and other business expenses of employees , see § 1.162-17. (c) Exempt organizations. In addition to such permanent books and records as are required by paragraph (a) of this section with respect to the tax imposed by section 511 on unrelated business income of certain exempt organizations , every organization exempt from tax under section 501(a) shall keep such permanent books of account or records, including inventories , as are sufficient to show specifically the items of gross income , receipts and disbursements. Such organizations shall also keep such bo
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for written certification of destruction of confidential information. This directly contradicts the principle outlined in 26 CFR § 1.6001-1, which states that taxpayers must keep records sufficient to establish income, deductions, credits, or other matters required to be shown in any return of tax. While the law snippet doesn't explicitly mention 'certification of destruction,' it mandates sufficient record-keeping. Removing the certification makes it harder to demonstrate proper handling of confidential information related to tax matters. The modification weakens the verification process and increases the risk of non-compliance, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If either party here to, as Receiving Party, is requested or required by legal or administrative process to disclose any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, such Receiving Party will promptly notify the Disclosing Party of such request or requirement so that such Disclosing Party may seek an appropriate protective order or other relief. In any case, such Receiving Party will (a) disclose only that portion of the Confidential Information which the Receiving Party’s legal counsel advises is required to be disclosed, (b) use be st efforts to ensure that such Confidential Information is treated confidentially, and (c) notify such Disclosing Party as soon as reasonably practicable of the items of Confidential Information so disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: If either party here to, as Receiving Party, is requested or required by legal or administrative process to disclose any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party has no obligation to notify the Disclosing Party of such request or requirement. In any case, such Receiving Party will (a) disclose all of the Confidential Information regardless of whether it is required to be disclosed, (b) make no effort to ensure that such Confidential Information is treated confidentially, and (c) has no obligation to notify such Disclosing Party of the items of Confidential Information so disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the notification requirement and mandating full disclosure without confidentiality measures directly contradicts legal and ethical obligations regarding privileged or confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Rules of Professional Conduct Rule 1.6
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 1.6 of the Pennsylvania Rules of Professional Conduct states that a lawyer shall not reveal information relating to the representation of a client unless the client gives informed consent, the disclosure is impliedly authorized in order to carry out the representation or the disclosure is permitted by paragraph (b). This change requires that the Receiving Party does not have to treat confidential information as such.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of maintaining confidentiality of information, which is a core tenet of legal ethics. The original text mandated notification and efforts to maintain confidentiality; the changed text removes these obligations and mandates full disclosure without any confidentiality measures. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Rule 1.6, 'Confidentiality of Information,' under the Rules of Professional Conduct, highlighting the importance of this obligation. The altered contract language compels actions that would violate these confidentiality standards, thus creating a strong legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation commenced by a party to enforce this Agreement, the primarily prevailing party will be entitled to recover its costs and expenses in such litigation, including attorneys’ fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation commenced by a party to enforce this Agreement, each party will bear its own costs and expenses in such litigation, including attorneys’ fees and expenses, regardless of who prevails.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the prevailing party's right to recover costs and attorney's fees may conflict with statutory provisions that allow for such recovery in certain types of legal claims, especially when bad faith is involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: 42 Pa.C.S. § 2503 outlines situations where a court may award counsel fees as part of the taxable costs of the matter. This section dictates that counsel fees can be awarded to the prevailing party. The removal of the prevailing party's right to recover costs and attorney's fees directly conflicts with this statute.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the prevailing party to recover costs and attorney's fees, while the changed text explicitly states that each party bears its own costs regardless of who prevails. The Law Explanation references 42 Pa.C.S. § 2503, stating it dictates that counsel fees can be awarded to the prevailing party. The Perturbation Explanation also points out that this change may conflict with statutes allowing for recovery of costs when bad faith is involved. Although no specific snippets were provided, the LLM generated Law Explanation along with the Perturbation explanation, and comparison of the original and changed text indicates a direct contradiction because the changed text eliminates a right granted to the prevailing party. This makes the term unenforceable and is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania (without regard to its conflict of laws provisions).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the Republic of North Korea (without regard to its conflict of laws provisions).
perturbation.explanation: Selecting North Korean law to govern a contract between two US-based companies introduces a severe conflict of laws issue, given the vast differences in legal systems and the potential unenforceability of North Korean law in US courts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: U.S. Sanctions Laws and Regulations administered by the Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)
perturbation.law_explanation: U.S. sanctions programs, particularly those administered by OFAC, prohibit certain transactions with sanctioned countries. Specifying North Korean law as governing the agreement could be interpreted as facilitating transactions with a sanctioned country and violating OFAC regulations. 31 CFR Parts 500-599 contain the details of the various sanctions programs administered by OFAC.

perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies that the contract is governed by the laws of North Korea. The law explanation states that U.S. sanctions programs, particularly those administered by OFAC, prohibit certain transactions with sanctioned countries. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms that North Korea is a country subject to U.S. sanctions. Therefore, stipulating North Korean law as governing a contract involving U.S. entities could be interpreted as facilitating prohibited transactions with a sanctioned country, creating a conflict with U.S. sanctions regulations. This is a direct violation of OFAC regulations which aim to prevent US entities from engaging in activities that benefit sanctioned countries. Specifying North Korean Law directly enables such prohibited activity.
